title: Chapter 9: The Well
content: The ink was still wet on the last page when Helen Burns heard her editor's footsteps.

She'd been sitting at her desk for nine hours, surrounded by printouts arranged in a geometry that only made sense to her — corporate registrations, shipping manifests, a web of arrows drawn in red pen connecting names to names to names. The office smelled of toner and cold coffee and the particular staleness of a building where the heating clicked off at eight. Around her, the night shift hummed: a police scanner crackling at the city desk, someone's keyboard clattering in the far corner, the fluorescent lights buzzing their idiot frequency.
Helen didn't look up. She was staring at the Global Sustainable Textile Initiative.

GSTI. Registered in Valletta, Malta. Website live for fourteen months — she'd checked the Wayback Machine, and before that, nothing. No cached pages, no press mentions, no conference appearances, no presence at any of the major sustainability summits. The site was competent but thin: stock photos of cotton fields, a mission statement about "transparent supply chains," a list of board members. Three of them. Sandra Grünfeld. Marcus Grünfeld. Pieter Grünfeld.
She turned to the other document on her desk — a photocopied page from Thornfield's most recent annual filing, obtained through a contact at Companies House. The CFO's name: Henrik Grünfeld.

Four Grünfelds. Three on the board of a certifying body, one signing the checks at the company being certified. The organic cotton from Senegal — Thornfield's flagship sustainability claim, the line that appeared in every editorial spread and every brand manifesto — was vouchsafed by the CFO's own family.
Shell inside a shell. A wound doll of corporate nonsense.

Helen hit send on the encrypted email to Jane, attaching everything: the Malta registration, the Wayback timeline, the Grünfeld genealogy she'd scraped from LinkedIn and a Dutch civil records database. The subject line read: *Follow the cotton. Then follow the name.*
She leaned back. The chair groaned. She pressed her palms against her eyes until the fluorescent afterimage dissolved into darkness.
"Burns."
She dropped her hands. Her editor stood in the cubicle opening, a legal-sized envelope in one hand and an expression that meant the conversation was already over before it started.
"The Thornfield story is on hold."
Helen's stomach dropped. "On hold?"
"Legal sent a letter this afternoon. We're being sued."
"By Thornfield."

"By their parent company." He turned the envelope so she could see the letterhead through the transparent window. A Luxembourg address. A firm she'd never heard of — probably because it existed for exactly this purpose. "They're claiming defamation, interference with business relations, and — this is the creative part — commercial espionage."
"I haven't published anything."
"They're saying your research constitutes prior restraint on their trade practices. The letter's forty pages. Legal wants us to stand down until they've reviewed it."
"Forty pages from a company registered in Luxembourg." Helen heard her own voice go flat. "That's not a lawsuit. That's a threat dressed in Latin."
Her editor looked at her with something that was almost sympathy. Almost. "I know. But the paper can't fight this right now. Not without a published story to defend. You understand."
She understood perfectly. Institutional silence. The oldest play in the book: you don't have to win the case. You just have to make the case expensive enough that no one dares to file it.
Her editor walked away. The envelope stayed on the corner of her desk like a dead thing.
Helen picked up her red pen and drew a line from the Luxembourg entity to the Malta registry. Then another line, from Malta, back to Henrik Grünfeld. The web grew. The center held.
She did not stop working.
Two days at the château, and Jane had become fluent in the language of pleasant compliance.

She worked in the atelier each morning alongside the small team, cataloging textile samples with a methodical care that drew compliments from Grace Poole, of all people. She laughed at the right moments over lunch. She wore the Thornfield coat each evening — the weight of it still strange on her shoulders, her grandmother's brooch fastened at the collar like a small act of defiance disguised as sentiment. She smiled at Edward across the dinner table, and when he asked about her progress, she gave him the answer he wanted: the archive was impeccably maintained, the collection was extraordinary, she was learning a great deal.
She did not mention the HELP codes stitched into the lining of Room 2.
She did not mention the file labeled MASON, BERTHA — or the password lock that had turned her away, then trapped her in the dark.

Instead, she built. A dossier in her head, cross-referenced with the hidden photo album on her phone. She'd photographed the red thread from Room 2 before it went into the evidence bag she kept zipped inside her suitcase. She'd photographed the HELP codes — H, E, L, P, each one in its precise position on the garment. She'd photographed the archive screen before the system locked her out, the name BERTHA MASON frozen there like a face at a window.
She moved through the château the way water moves through stone: without force, but with direction.

Dinner on the third night was poached pike with beurre blanc, a Sancerre so crisp it seemed to have edges, and candles in iron sconces that turned the dining room into something from a painting — all amber and shadow and the low murmur of conversation that meant nothing and everything.

Adèle had been excused early, carried off by the governess with a yawn and a half-eaten madeleine. Blanche sat at the far end of the table, her dark hair swept up, her collarbones sharp above the neckline of a dress Jane recognized from the Spring 2024 collection — Blanche wore Thornfield the way other people wore armor. Grace Poole had taken her meal in her room, as she often did. Only four remained: Jane, Edward, Blanche, and a visiting buyer from Milan whose name Jane had already forgotten and who was absorbed in his phone.
Jane let the silence hold for a count of five. Then: "The biodegradable silk is remarkable."
Edward looked up from his plate. "You've been spending time with the current collection?"

"A little. The hand-feel is exceptional. But what struck me was the construction." She paused, timed it like a breath. "Specifically, the seam allowance. It's been maximized — wider than any standard I've seen in sustainable production. Almost as if the fabric were designed to be... deconstructed later."
She said it casually. The way you'd mention the weather, or a painting you'd glanced at in a hallway.
Edward's hand froze on his wine glass.
One beat. The same beat she'd caught in the corridor outside Room 2 — that fractional suspension, a mechanism catching on a gear. His fingers tightened around the stem, then relaxed. He smiled.
"Innovation requires... unconventional methods."

The word landed like a stone in still water. *Unconventional.* She'd heard it before. That exact word, that same slight pause before it, as though he were selecting it from a drawer of acceptable alternatives.
Jane filed it. Second occurrence. Pattern confirmed.

"It's beautiful work," she said, and meant it — which was the most dangerous part. Whatever was wrong with Thornfield, the craft was real. The hands that made these garments knew what they were doing. Someone in this house possessed a talent that bordered on the obsessive, and that someone was not the man across from her, and they both knew it.
She let a moment pass. Then another.

"The construction in those pieces reminds me of something." She turned her wine glass slowly by the stem. "The hand threading — it's distinctive. Almost like a signature. Does the house have a principal couturier? Someone whose work I might recognize from another atelier?"
Edward's smile did not waver. But his eyes went flat — the way a lake goes flat before the surface tension breaks.

"Thornfield is a house, Jane. Not a person." He spoke carefully, each word placed with the precision of a man reading from a script he'd rehearsed too many times. "The work is collective. That's our philosophy — collective brilliance, individual discretion."
Jane held his gaze for precisely one second longer than was comfortable.

"Collective brilliance, individual discretion." She repeated it back to him softly, as if tasting the words. "From your Spring 2023 manifesto. Page four." She let the corner of her mouth lift — not a smile, something closer to recognition. "Nice phrasing, by the way. Very... convenient."
The word hung between them like smoke.
*Convenient.* A word that meant: I've read your script. I've memorized your moves. And I'm still sitting at your table.

Edward's eyes changed. Not anger — something more interesting than anger. Recalibration. The look of a man updating his model of an opponent mid-game. He leaned back in his chair, the wine glass still held loosely in one hand, and regarded her with an attention she hadn't felt from him before.
"You're thorough," he said.
"I read labels."

The double meaning slid across the table like the blade it was. He knew — she could see it in the slight narrowing of his eyes — that she wasn't talking about fabric tags. She was talking about labels of every kind: the ones sewn into garments, the ones applied to people, the ones that said *organic* and *sustainable* and *collective* when what they really meant was *invisible.*

For a long moment, neither of them spoke. The candle flames stood upright and still. The Sancerre breathed in their glasses. Somewhere below the floor, below the foundation stones, the château held its silence.
Then Edward smiled — a real one this time, or something closer to real. "I'm glad you're here, Jane."
The Milanese buyer laughed at something on his phone. The sound was absurdly normal.
At the far end of the table, Blanche caught Jane's eye. No smile. No flicker of amusement. Just a look — brief, deliberate, weighted — that said: *Be careful.*
Jane held it for one beat. Then she looked away.
After dinner, she lay in her room with the lights off and the phone pressed to her chest, waiting.

The signal at the château was capricious — a bar or two that surfaced like a gasp between the thick stone walls, then vanished. She'd learned to hold the device near the window at certain hours, patient as a fisherman, and wait for the invisible world to rush in.

At eleven forty-three, it came. The phone buzzed against her sternum — a single, electric pulse, and then the download began. Three percent. Seven. The signal flickered, steadied. Twelve percent. She didn't breathe. Twenty. Forty. The phone grew warm in her palm. Then the attachment cracked open like a shell, and Helen's email spilled out across the screen.
She read it by the phone's blue glow, the light making a small bright cave of her face in the dark room.

The GSTI certification was a front. The board — Sandra, Marcus, Pieter Grünfeld — were relatives of Thornfield's CFO. The organic cotton from Senegal had origin records that didn't match the shipping manifests. The ports were wrong. The tonnage was wrong. Whatever was arriving at the Loire Valley facility, it wasn't what the labels said it was.

Jane scrolled to the corporate entity map Helen had drawn. Three Grünfeld-named companies, each holding a piece of the supply chain: certification, procurement, logistics. A closed circuit. Money flowing from Thornfield to GSTI to Grünfeld Logistics back to Thornfield, each pass scrubbing the cotton's origin a little cleaner, each pass adding a layer of plausible deniability.

It wasn't proof of sweatshop labor. Not yet. But it was proof of fraud. The entire sustainability architecture — the press releases, the manifestos, the word *organic* stamped on every label — was built on a foundation of shell companies and family signatures. A house of cards dressed in silk.
Jane closed the email and stared at the ceiling. The phone's screen dimmed, then went dark.

Helen had the money trail. Jane had the thread trail — the red thread, the HELP codes, the hands that made the clothes that no one was allowed to name. Two lines of inquiry, running parallel, and somewhere ahead they would intersect. They had to. The fraud and the forced anonymity were not separate crimes; they were the same crime, viewed from different angles. The architecture of disappearance.
She thought of Edward's eyes going flat when she asked about a principal couturier. *Collective brilliance, individual discretion.* The most elegant way she'd ever heard someone erased.
She thought of the HELP codes. H-E-L-P. A woman who could not speak, stitching her message into the only thing she was allowed to touch.
She thought of the file labeled MASON, BERTHA, locked behind a password she couldn't crack in a system that had locked her in the dark.

And she thought — with a certainty that settled in her chest like a stone dropped into a well — of what lay beneath her feet. Below the atelier. Below the archive. Below the foundation stones of this beautiful, burning house.
A woman was waiting.
Two trails converged in the dark, and at the point where they met, a name she could not yet speak.
*Bertha.*
